learning_objectives: Articulate why data literacy is essential for modern communications professionals
Identify the types of data generated by digital communications activities
Distinguish between descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive analytics
readings: Davenport & Kim, Keeping Up with the Quants, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Data analytics transforms communications from guesswork into evidence-based practice. This course teaches you to collect, analyze, visualize, and interpret data from digital communications channels, turning numbers into narratives that drive strategic decisions.
key_concepts: Analytics maturity: descriptive (what happened), diagnostic (why), predictive (what will happen), prescriptive (what to do)
Communications data sources: web analytics, social metrics, email metrics, CRM data, survey data
Quantitative vs. qualitative data: metrics and numbers vs. sentiment, feedback, and open-ended responses
Data-driven vs. data-informed: using data to guide decisions while retaining human judgment and context
The analytics workflow: question, collect, clean, analyze, visualize, interpret, recommend, act
lecture_notes.demonstration: Examining a real communications challenge (declining email open rates) and walking through the analytics workflow from question definition to recommendation, using actual data sets
setup_requirements: Google Sheets or Excel for data exercises
Google Data Studio (Looker Studio) for visualization

review.value: Descriptive analytics tells you what happened, diagnostic analytics tells you why, predictive analytics forecasts what will happen, and prescriptive analytics recommends what to do about it; most communications teams operate at descriptive and diagnostic levels
Using data as one input alongside context, experience, and strategic judgment, rather than blindly following numbers; data without interpretation is noise, and numbers without context can mislead

class_schedule.topic: Web Analytics: Google Analytics Deep Dive
learning_objectives: Navigate Google Analytics 4 to extract meaningful communications insights
Set up events, conversions, and audiences for communications measurement
Build custom reports and explorations to answer specific communications questions
readings: Google Analytics Academy, 'Google Analytics 4' certification course (modules 1-3)

lecture_notes.introduction: Google Analytics is the most widely used web analytics platform in the world. Mastering GA4's event-based model allows you to measure virtually any user interaction on a website, connecting communications efforts to measurable business outcomes.
key_concepts: GA4 event-based model: every interaction is an event (page_view, click, scroll, purchase)
Dimensions and metrics: dimensions describe data (page path, source), metrics measure it (sessions, users, conversion rate)
Custom events: tracking specific interactions relevant to communications (video plays, PDF downloads, form starts)
Exploration reports: free-form analysis beyond standard reports for custom questions
User segments and audiences: defining groups based on behavior for targeted analysis
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a custom exploration in GA4 to answer: 'Which blog posts drive the most email newsletter sign-ups, and what traffic sources bring the highest-converting readers?'
setup_requirements: Google Analytics 4 demo account (Google Merchandise Store)

review.value: GA4 treats every user interaction as an event with parameters, replacing the session-based pageview model; this allows tracking of any measurable interaction, from scroll depth to video engagement to button clicks

GA4's advanced analysis tool for building custom queries with drag-and-drop dimensions and metrics, enabling communications professionals to answer specific questions beyond what standard reports provide

learning_objectives: Select appropriate chart types for different data stories
Build interactive dashboards in Google Data Studio (Looker Studio)
Apply data visualization best practices to make reports clear and actionable

lecture_notes.introduction: The ability to visualize data clearly is what makes analytics actionable. A well-designed chart communicates a finding in seconds; a poorly designed one obscures it. Data visualization is not decoration. It is communication.
key_concepts: Chart selection: bar charts for comparison, line charts for trends, pie charts sparingly, scatter plots for relationships
Visual decluttering: removing chart junk, gridlines, borders, and 3D effects that add no information
Color as information: using color intentionally to highlight key data, not for decoration
Dashboard design: KPIs at the top, trends in the middle, details at the bottom; one question per dashboard section
Data storytelling: structuring a report as a narrative with context, findings, and recommended actions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete communications dashboard in Looker Studio: connecting to Google Analytics and social media data, creating KPI scorecards, trend charts, and a channel comparison table with appropriate formatting
setup_requirements: Sample data source connections (Google Analytics, Google Sheets)

review.value: Visual elements in charts that convey no data (3D effects, excessive gridlines, decorative images, gradient fills); removing chart junk improves data-ink ratio and makes the actual data more readable

Visual properties (color, size, position, shape) that the brain processes before conscious attention; using these deliberately in data visualization draws the viewer's eye to the most important data point immediately

learning_objectives: Extract and analyze data from social media platform analytics
Interpret email marketing metrics to optimize campaign performance
Combine data from multiple sources to build a holistic communications picture

lecture_notes.introduction: Each communications channel generates its own data stream. The real power of analytics emerges when you combine data from social media, email, web, and other channels to understand how they work together and where to allocate resources.
key_concepts: Social media metrics hierarchy: vanity (followers, likes), engagement (rate, shares, saves), conversion (clicks, sign-ups, purchases)
Email analytics: open rate, click-through rate, click-to-open rate, conversion rate, unsubscribe rate, list growth rate
Cross-channel analysis: connecting social media engagement to website traffic to email sign-ups to conversions
Cohort analysis: tracking how groups of users acquired at the same time behave over time
Benchmarking: comparing performance against industry averages, competitors, and your own historical data
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a cross-channel analysis: tracing a content campaign from social media posts through website visits to email sign-ups and final conversions, using UTM parameters and multi-source dashboard
setup_requirements: Sample social media analytics exports (provided on LMS)
Sample email marketing data (provided on LMS)

review.value: The percentage of email recipients who clicked a link among those who opened the email; a more accurate measure of content effectiveness than click-through rate, which is diluted by unopened emails

Grouping users by a shared characteristic (acquisition date, source, first action) and tracking their behavior over time, revealing trends that aggregate data obscures, such as whether engagement quality is improving or declining

learning_objectives: Design a proper A/B test with hypothesis, control, variant, and success metric
Calculate statistical significance and determine when a test has reached valid conclusions
Apply experimentation practices to email, landing page, and social media optimization
readings: Kohavi, Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: A/B testing is the scientific method applied to communications. Instead of debating which headline is better, you test both with real audiences and let the data decide. Experimentation culture replaces opinion-based decision making with evidence-based optimization.
key_concepts: Hypothesis formation: 'If we change X, then Y will improve because Z'
Test design: control (original), variant (changed version), one variable at a time
Sample size requirements: calculating the traffic or audience needed for statistically valid results
Statistical significance: the probability (typically 95%) that the result is not due to random chance
Common tests: email subject lines, landing page headlines, CTA button text, social media post timing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing and analyzing three A/B tests: an email subject line test, a landing page headline test, and a social media CTA test, calculating significance and interpreting results
setup_requirements: A/B test sample size calculator (Optimizely or VWO)
Sample test data for analysis (provided on LMS)

review.value: The confidence level (typically 95%) that an observed difference between control and variant is real and not due to random variation; running a test too short or on too small a sample produces unreliable results

Changing only one element at a time between control and variant so that any performance difference can be attributed to that specific change; testing multiple changes simultaneously makes it impossible to know which caused the effect

questions.question: Describe the four levels of analytics maturity (descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, prescriptive) and give an example of each applied to email marketing.
questions.answer: Descriptive: 'Our email open rate was 22% last month.' Diagnostic: 'Open rates dropped because we sent 3 emails in one week, causing subscriber fatigue.' Predictive: 'Based on historical patterns, sending emails on Tuesday mornings will yield 25% open rates next month.' Prescriptive: 'Reduce email frequency to 2 per week, personalize subject lines based on past click behavior, and A/B test send times to maximize engagement.' Each level builds on the previous one, progressing from reporting what happened to recommending what to do about it.

rubric.key: Shows how levels build on each other

questions.question: In Google Analytics 4, explain what events are and why the event-based model is more flexible than the previous session-based model for measuring communications effectiveness.
questions.answer: In GA4, events are any user interaction on a website: page views, clicks, scrolls, video plays, file downloads, and custom interactions. The event-based model is more flexible than the session-based model because it treats every interaction as a measurable data point with customizable parameters, rather than limiting measurement to pageviews within sessions. For communications, this means you can track exactly how users engage with specific content elements (how far they scroll, which videos they watch, which CTAs they click) without pageviews as the primary unit. This provides much richer engagement data for evaluating content effectiveness.

rubric.key: Defines events correctly in GA4 context

questions.question: What is click-to-open rate in email analytics, and why is it a better indicator of content quality than click-through rate?
questions.answer: Click-to-open rate (CTOR) is the percentage of recipients who clicked a link among those who opened the email (clicks divided by opens, times 100). It is a better indicator of content quality than click-through rate (CTR, which divides clicks by total recipients) because CTOR isolates the email body content performance from the subject line performance. A low CTR could mean the subject line failed (people did not open) even though the content was compelling, or the content failed even though the subject line was great. CTOR removes the subject line variable, measuring only whether the people who read the email found the content compelling enough to click.

rubric.key: Explains why it isolates content quality
Contrasts with CTR and identifies the limitation CTR has

questions.question: Describe the one-variable rule in A/B testing and explain what goes wrong when you test multiple changes simultaneously.
questions.answer: The one-variable rule states that an A/B test should change only one element between the control and variant versions so that any difference in performance can be attributed to that specific change. When multiple changes are tested simultaneously (e.g., changing both the headline and the CTA button color on a landing page), it is impossible to determine which change caused the performance difference. The headline might have improved conversion while the button color hurt it, but the net result obscures both effects. This makes the test useless for learning, even if the variant wins, because you cannot replicate the insight on other pages.

rubric.key: Notes that even winning variants teach nothing if multiple variables changed

major_assignments.title: Communications Analytics Dashboard and Insights Report
major_assignments.description: Build an interactive analytics dashboard combining data from web analytics, social media, and email marketing for a real or simulated organization. Deliver an accompanying insights report that translates the data into strategic communications recommendations.
objectives: Connect and visualize data from multiple communications channels
Design a dashboard that enables stakeholders to monitor KPIs at a glance
Analyze cross-channel data to identify patterns, opportunities, and problems
Translate data findings into actionable communications recommendations
deliverables: Interactive dashboard in Looker Studio with data from at least three sources
Insights report (6-8 pages) with data-driven findings and recommendations
A/B test proposal with hypothesis, design, and success criteria for the top recommendation
Executive summary (1 page) with key metrics, findings, and priority actions

criteria: Dashboard connects to at least three data sources
Visualizations are appropriate for the data types and questions
Layout follows dashboard design best practices (KPIs, trends, details)
Cross-channel analysis identifies genuine patterns and relationships
Analysis goes beyond descriptive to diagnostic and prescriptive levels
Findings are supported by specific data points and comparisons
Benchmarking provides appropriate context for performance evaluation
Recommendations are specific, actionable, and grounded in data
A/B test proposal is properly designed with hypothesis and success criteria
Priority actions are justified by projected impact
Executive summary communicates effectively to non-technical stakeholders
Professional dashboard demonstrating data integration and visualization skills
Rigorous cross-channel analysis with genuine diagnostic and prescriptive insights
Actionable recommendations grounded in data with sound A/B test design
Clear executive communication of complex analytical findings

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on analytics concepts, tools, and interpretation

weekly_analytics_labs.description: Weekly hands-on analytics exercises in Google Analytics, Looker Studio, and spreadsheets

major_assignment.description: Cross-channel analytics dashboard with insights report

midterm_examination.description: Practical examination including data analysis and visualization

final_examination.description: Cumulative examination with live data analysis and strategic recommendations

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions. Analytics tools require hands-on practice that cannot be replicated outside class. More than 3 absences result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Dashboard projects submitted late lose 15% per day since peer review depends on timely delivery.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed in pairs. All graded assignments are individual. Sharing dashboards, data analyses, or reports between students is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All data must come from stated sources. Fabricating or manipulating analytics data is academic fraud. Screenshots and data exports must be authentic and unaltered.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Google Analytics access, Google Data Studio (Looker Studio) account, Google Sheets, and a laptop with reliable internet. Optional: Excel or Google Sheets proficiency for data manipulation.
